comments.content:
Hello JMG,
I've decided to make another dreamwidth account to talk about the Heathen Golden Dawn, as I'd still like to be relatively anonymous for the moment, and it can be a little confusing with multiple anonymous posters.
1. So, in regards to the Heathen Temple Opening and Closing, as I've been practicing the basic Polytheist one you've already published, I've only amended that slightly.
I can write it up step by step if you approve, but it starts by saying "in the name of the Aesir and Vanir", when I purify with water I say "Let this temple and all within it be purified with the blood of Ymir" and when I consecrate with fire I say "Let this temple and all within it be consecrated by the sparks of Muspell." And with the water, I sprinkle it in the sign of the hammer, and I make the sign of the hammer with the incense too. I originally wanted to invoke the waters of Hvergelmir and the fire of Muspellheim, but got a negative reading from the runes. The blood of Ymir and the sparks of Muspell are what the Gods used to create this world, but the primal spring and the primal fire themselves are probably too much for the human world. 

Then, I only circumambulate 3 times instead of 4. Otherwise, it's pretty much the same.
Does that sound alright?
2. So I found out that, even though the copy of Havamal that I saw the word was spelled "Ginregin", it's actually more appropriately spelled "Ginnregin", which has 9 letters. Yeah, "Ginregin" has 8. I counted it several times when I was working on the hexagram ritual, but somehow missed that. I felt very very stupid for a while, then asked the gods and the runes what to do, and the runes said basically that I should stick with the hexagram ritual,  and use "Ginnregin", at least for the time being-  In heathenry, 9 letters is not insignificant, and the way the word sounds is also very powerful. What is your advice? I asked a friend who is a old norse specialist about possible 7 letter words for all the gods and found one or two, but they don't have the ring.

3. In general, how much would you use divination in working on a system like this? Would you run everything by divination, or more just use reason to see how things fit together? What if something fits together really well on many levels but the divination wasn't favorable? How often is divination wrong?
4. I have been thinking alot about an Enneagram ritual, for the obvious 9 symbolism, do you think that could be a useful addition to this system?

5. I also realized that this is kind of a huge project, and got a little overwhelmed, realizing that I need to get more serious than I have been about studying. I feel a little daunted, but also feel like I am capable of doing this. I'm also really excited and have lots and lots of ideas.

replies.content:
1) This sounds great.

2) Use it. It has a good sound and vibrates well, and since the original spelling didn't have seven letters either, nine isn't that big a deal.

3) I tend to use divination only when I really need it -- say, when I have two alternatives and can't choose between them. A combination of intuition and systematic field testing does just as well.

4) What are the nine powers you would invoke with it?

5) Excellent. Go for it.

replies.content:
I'd recommend scrying the runes, and in general putting the runes to work in every suitable context -- they're a fine magical alphabet and have had a lot of people using them in recent decades, so they'll have plenty of power. I like the idea of talking with Hugin and Munin! As for a complete system, if it's got ritual, meditation, and divination it's got all the basics of esoteric training; you can have initiates study the Voluspa and the Gylfaginning, and indeed meditate on them, as good classic sources for Norse symbolism; you've got the Nine Herbs poem as a foundation for basic natural magic; so it's sounding pretty good.

You'll need to decide what the magical working tools of the system are -- Sven and I talked about putting a hammer and a ring on the altar and moving it to different positions for the degrees of the system, but you'll need some other hardware: something like a wand, something to wear around the neck, and a piece of sharp steel to deal with hostile critters, as a bare minimum. Beyond that? Well, start working toward the more complex rituals and see what suggests itself. My guess is that the gods will guide you.

Some thoughts to consider on the tools, if you like:

- Wand: wands (gands) seem to be pretty traditional, so this likely works from a lore standpoint
- Something to wear around the neck: a Thor's Hammer makes good sense, and you can make a decent one with some very rudimentary wood carving (I bought a small 1/4" thick oak board from a wood working shop, traced a hammer shape onto it, and then did a bit of carving with a v-shaped chisel to decorate). Another option might be similar to the "Crane Bag" from DMH (not sure if it's in CGD), but with the runes or other symbolic objects/herbs
- Piece of sharp steel: A saex or rune rister would make sense here. If going for a rune rister, a nice woodcarving/chip knife would likely be a good choice. A saex might be a bit tougher, but there are lots of nice replicas out there.

replies.content:
For those that would like to practice the Heathen Temple Opening & Closing, I've added it below. I'll also post it on my dreamwidth account.

Opening the Temple

First, stand at the west side of the altar, facing east. Raise your right hand, palm forward, to salute the divine powers you will summon during the ritual, and say, “In the name of the Aesir and Vanir, I prepare to open this temple.”

Second, perform the complete Lesser Invoking Ritual of the Pentagram to call magical energies into the space.

Third, standing at the west of the altar, facing east, pick up the vessel of water in both hands and raise it up. Say: “Let this temple and all within it be purified with the blood of Ymir.” Go to the east, dip the fingers of one hand into the water, and flick droplets of water in the sign of the Hammer, using three motions, to the east. Go around to the south, and do the same thing; repeat the same action in the west and the north. Return to the east, face east, lift up the vessel of water in both hands, and say: “The temple is purified.” Then go back to the west of the altar and return the vessel of water to its place.

Fourth, standing at the west of the altar facing east, pick up the incense—if you are burning stick incense, just the stick is fine; if you are using cone or loose incense, lift up the burner in both hands. Say, “Let this temple and all within it be consecrated by the sparks of Muspell.” Go to the east and with one hand, draw the sign of the Hammer in smoke to the east. Go around to the south, and do the same thing; repeat the same action in the west and the north. Return to the east, face east, lift up the incense in both hands, and say: “The temple is consecrated.” Then go back to the west of the altar and return the incense to its place.

Fifth, starting from the west of the altar, walk clockwise in a circle around the altar, passing the east three times. Each time you pass the east, bow your head in respect. This is the ancient and very widespread Pagan rite of circumambulation. As you walk, imagine your movements creating a whirlpool of energy that draws in magical power from the far reaches of the universe to your magical temple. When you have passed the east three times, circle back to the west of the altar and face east.

Sixth, spread your arms wide , so your body looks like the rune Elhaz, and say, “Aesir and Vanir, holy gods, I ask you to bless and consecrate this temple of high magic, and aid me with your power in all the work I perform herein.” Pause for a time, and concentrate on sensing your patron deity’s presence and power surrounding you.

Seventh, still standing at the west of the altar facing east, raise your right hand again, palm forward, and say, “In the name of the Aesir and Vanir, and in the presence of all the gods and goddesses, I proclaim this temple duly open.” This completes the ritual.

Closing the Temple:

First, standing at the west of the altar, facing east, pick up the vessel of water in both hands and raise it up. Say: “Let this temple and all within it be purified with the blood of Ymir.” Repeat the process of purifying the temple with water, exactly as you did in the third step of the opening ritual. Then go back to the west of the altar and return the vessel of water to its place.

Second, standing at the west of the altar facing east, pick up the incense and say, “Let this temple and all within it be consecrated by the sparks of Muspell.” Repeat the process of consecrating the temple with fire, exactly as you did in the fourth step of the opening ritual. Then go back to the west of the altar and return the incense to its place.

Third, starting from the west of the altar, walk counterclockwise in a circle around the altar, passing the east three times. Each time you pass the east, bow your head in respect. As you walk, imagine your movements dispersing the whirlpool of energy you created earlier and sending the intention of your working out into the universe to accomplish your will. When you have passed the east three times, circle back to the west of the altar and face east.

Fourth, spread your arms wide so your body looks like the rune Elhaz and say, “ In the name of the Aesir and Vanir, I set free any spirits who may have been imprisoned by this ceremony. Depart unto your rightful habitations in peace, and peace be between us.” Pause for a moment, and then perform the complete Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram.

Fifth, standing at the west of the altar facing east, raise your right hand again, palm forward, and say, “In the name of Aesir and Vanir, and in the presence of all the gods and goddesses, I proclaim this temple duly closed.” This completes the ritual.

comments.title: Lamp Update, Affirmation vs Prayer, Cross-Checking Divination, Iron Watch, Tinne
comments.content:
Hello and thank you as always for sharing your knowledge and providing this forum.

A quick update, especially for open_space: I found a lovely ceramic oil lamp and am going to give it a try this week in place of candles for daily prayer. I'll be experimenting with different oils, using tissue paper to tint the color of the light, and possibly with adding essential oils to the lamp oil, and I'll share how it goes here.

Questions:
1. A question on affirmations vs prayer, with a bit of set up to make clear: some time back, I distilled various sources meaningful to me into 9 virtues. Recently, with some very helpful guidance from Pagan Prayer Beads, I made a pocket set of prayer beads to call on a different deity for each virtue, with the goal of better living up to these virtues day-to-day. Here's the question: for writing the prayer, should I follow the advice for affirmations (short, simple, present-tense)? As an example to make things clearer, would it be better to pray something like "Heimdall, Watchful Warder, please teach me to have Grit" or "Heimdall, Watchful Warder, from you I learn Grit"?
2. Does it mess up learning one divination method to "cross-check" an answer by asking the same question of a better-understood method?
3. Is it helpful or appropriate to use discursive meditation to think through non-spiritual matters, or might that foul up the channels being opened for spiritual work?
4. I usually wear a watch with a stainless steel body and band - should I take it off when doing magic due to iron's etheric "shorting" qualities?
5. Over the course of one week (the Wednesday before last to last Wednesday), I drew Tinne four times in various positions for my daily divination (3 times reversed, once upright). Other than that I should be meditating more on conflict/challenge/balance/building skill, is there a wider trend than the daily significance I should be understanding here?

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Lamp Update, Affirmation vs Prayer, Cross-Checking Divination, Iron Watch, Tinne
replies.content:
1) Prayers and affirmations are not the same thing. In a prayer you're speaking to an actual conscious being; phrase it accordingly.

2) It can. It also shows distrust, which is not helpful.

3) Now and again it's not harmful, but it's not a good idea to make a habit of it -- one of the goals of meditation is to shift the focus of your mind to the spiritual.

4) Yes.

5) Just that it was something you really needed to pay attention to last week!

replies.content: Happy to hear this! Given that those are available these days in the US experimenting should be easy, if you can get them from other place than the yuppie store, you'll save some buck.

I was, at the time, generally unmoved by traditional Christian religious iconography. However, on this occasion I had a profound spiritual experience while looking at this drawing. Tears were streaming down my face. Today I can recognize all kinds of powerful symbolism in it, but at the time I was fairly clueless.

More recently, I am having similar reactions to some of the artwork in Jung's Red Book. My working hypothesis is that he too was creating magical talismans.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's much bigger than your personality, and far more complex than any human personality ever can be. It contains--among many other things--very many alternate, similar personalities that a well-functioning human being swaps out with one another to deal competently with the ever-changing circumstances of a human life. There can also be, and usually are, other personalities there, too, which one had better never let out of the dungeon in which one keeps them shut away. (Most of us never even notice that we're constantly swapping one variant personality for another.)

It contains modes of perception that do not make use of the mechanisms of the material body at all, and that most people have been trained in childhood never to notice. It appears to contain portals into other places that are quite outside of our own universe (as physicists understand it). Some of these "other places" seem to be empty, while others are inhabited by various non-human or inhuman beings--some of them even seem to be predatory.

And it probably contains many other things as well, which I have not yet managed to understand even dimly, and surely never will manage to understand in the course of this ephemeral lifetime of mine. Also, there are real dragons here and there, and the heedless random wanderer may well get "eaten" by one while wandering.

I suspect that its structure cannot be entirely grasped by our very limited human minds, and any model of that structure we might construct will become more and more inconsistent logically as it becomes more and more adequate to the nature of the unconsciousness itself. Among other things, it may transcend the human contrast between sentient and non-sentient ways of being.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you Robert! That's a fascinating take on things. So the subconscious seem to be related to the Astral plane, or at least that is the closest that I can manage to describe it --it reminded me of Atkinson's The Astral World . I found fascinating what you said about the multiple personalities. I've heard a mystic I follow once say of certain modes of consciousness as "being a conscious schizophrenic" wonder if that is what he meant. I wonder too if any occultist has studied and documented the intersection of mental illness and occultism. It seems to me given some examples that insane people can access modes of consciousness they are not ready for because something broke in their minds. Have you heard about this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You're very welcome, open space!

The occult philosophy that I work with in my own life differs quite significantly from the one our host here uses. Mine doesn't posit the various planes at all, nor does it posit any long slow movement upwards through those planes towards some distant (and as yet barely graspable) future goal. Some of what other occultists treat by referring to those planes, I subsume under such things as portals and the "other places."

This is not to claim that one of us is right and the other wrong. Each of our different occult philosophies seems to me to be about as adequate (or inadequate) as the other in its map of realities far, far beyond the ability of humans to articulate, let alone understand.

Mine does not come from books, but from a wide range of unprinted sources, including my own experiences and family stories about the ancestors and their friends. I haven't found anything much like it in occult books and papers of the 20th century. Some of it is faintly echoed here and there in 19th-century books on Spiritualism and Mesmerism. It seems not to have been influenced even slightly by European or British occult thought. We don't have any unified family doctrines handed down over the generations, just a propensity for having odd or mystical experiences, for going hugely against the grain of whatever society we happen to be born into, and for placing very high value on stories of our ancestors.

My best overall take of it all is that we--all of us, humans and other beings--are merely tiny pawns on an incomprehensibly vast and complex gameboard, where even the mightiest Gods and Goddesses we ever can get a glimpse of are no more than (so to speak) kinghts, bishops and rooks. The individual game-players themselves are vaster and stranger than the entire universes that physicists study, and the game itself is being played among and between all those universes in a realm where neither time, space, mathematics or logic have any meaning. (In fact, these last-mentioned things serve, perhaps, as something like contamination barriers in bvirus labs: They, like the planetary gravity wells and the hard radiation of outer space, serve to keep us in isolation in our individual cosmic "petrie dishes.") And all those ultimste gsme-players may also be one single game-player, a pawn in its own right on some even vaster gameboard.

(For reference, this was my comment: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/140782.html?thread=17004270#cmt17004270)

I am reading Jung's Man and His Symbols, and in a burst of synchronicity I read this passage from Marie-Louise von Franz's chapter on individuation:

"The dark side of the Self is the most dangerous thing of all, precisely because the Self is the greatest power of the psyche. comments.title: Do Gods Evolve?
comments.content:
You've spoken and written that we reincarnate multiple time, evolving our life sparks, for a lack of a better word "soul", over the course of those many lifetime. Each time after death, reviewing the lessons we learn from the experiences of that lifetime. At some point we have learned enough to shake off our material body and continue our journey towards the spiritual plane. I believe you've also said that the spirits who have gone before us, serve as mentors and teachers during that pause, helping us to understand what we experienced.

This is what I've come to believe myself, from earlier study and now from what you have written in "Learning Ritual Magic".

Now if all souls evolve, do the souls which were birthed earlier than ours and which have passed beyond the material plane and become the spirits magicians deal with, continue to evolve as well? Are what we speak of as the Gods on the same journey?

I feel like it would be the height of human centric arrogance to think that we're the only ones evolving. That the Universe has put the Spirits/Gods there just for us but if they evolve and journey as well, then will a Spirit/God disappear from their place in the Universe.

If they do then, and I'm coming to the conclusion that they do, then is what we think of as a God, say the spirit we call Odin or Hekate, not a specific individual spirit but more like an office or a job title?

It seems to me that the spirit that was Hekate in 1000 BCE at the beginning of her worship in the Anatolia region of what is now Turkey isn't the same spirit that is now worshipped worldwide. That the Odin of medieval times is not the Odin of today. That the position that we think of as a God changes spiritual hands from time to time, perhaps as the shape of the civilization changes.

Or maybe what we think of as a God, isn't a single office but a conglomerate of spirits all performing the role of that God, adopting and using the same set of characteristics, duties and powers?

In reading on forums, the experiences of other magicians when they deal with same (assumed) spirit, they often have entirely different minor characteristics. Suggestions of what the God likes in the way of offerings can be radically different. Could this be because they are different spirits entirely, just working in the same job/office?

And that as those same spirits learn and grow from their interaction as gods, they leave and are replaced by newer ones?

Something I believe I read here, in a comment has stuck in my mind. The commenter had summoned a particular spirit. They asked it, "Are you (Name)?", to which it replied something like "We don't treat names as you do".

I've mediated on this several times, and feel like this "seems like the way things are" but I'd appreciate the your thoughts on it.

replies.title: Re: Do Gods Evolve?
replies.content: That's the understanding of deities you'll find in the Hindu Puranas, if I understand them correctly: Indra, say, is a title, not a specific soul, and in each age of the world a different soul takes on that role and fulfills its dharma. It seems quite sensible to me.

replies.title: Re: Do Gods Evolve?
replies.content:
Thanks, I'll have to look into the material on the Hindu Puranas then.

I have been a believer in reincarnation for most of my life, but only recently started to think more deeply on it. Some of the ramifications it you take it further than the shallow New Age fluff make for good subjects of meditation.

As for the Gods and how they fit into a expanded cosmos, I hang out in the shadows of several pagan and heathen FB groups. I find it interesting how almost every god out there, has been living since the beginning of time and is recognized as the most powerful and all knowing, according to those who worship that god. Seems like the "Lake Wobegon Pantheon" sometimes.

replies.title: Re: Do Gods Evolve?
replies.content:
That's an interesting theory. If the Gods do evolve, what are they evolving toward? Something like the Neoplatonic "The One"? Or something we humans can't conceive of?

I'll admit, though, that the many different, uh,"personalities" that seem to go by the names of Jesus, Odin, Zeus, etc., leaves me feeling a tad uncertain. I can't help but think that perhaps the reason Oliver Cromwell experienced a Jesus radically different from a Quaker's Jesus is that both experienced some projection of a divinized self-image or a psychospiritual aspect of themselves. Perhaps that makes no sense.

comments.title: Ethical Magic
comments.content:
Mr. Greer,

I must say you have caused me serious problems in my life. I used to be content to spend hours watching blobs of pixels moving across a glass screen, reading mainstream approved sci fi books, and trusting that the world was generally as presented to me on the aforementioned glass screen. My flying car was right around the corner. Now, I see the manipulation behind the commercials, the worldview being pushed by the writers, and that perhaps the nightly news is not as objective as I believed. I am now plagued by original thoughts and bored to tears by the once a week movie night my wife insists is "quality time" together. For shame, Mr. Greer, for shame!

Seriously though, thank you for being a light in the dark world we live in.

For a little background, I have a two year old and I managed to get my wife to agree that she should not watch TV until she is at least three years old (I pushed for five, but three was a compromise). I have a wonderfully imaginative little girl who takes trips to the ocean every day (she's been to the real one once), is remarkably empathetic (a kid pushes her and she feels sorry for that kid), and loves to read. Her grandmother was actually worried that she spends too much time with her books and not enough with her toys.

My wife and mother-in-law (who watches the kid frequently) and fully caught up in the glamour of the television and the materialist culture. My MiL is one those people for whom the TV has to always be on as "background noise". It took some convincing to get her to agree to turn it off when my daughter is around. Would doing a working to pierce the glamour be ethical (something similar to the placing a knife under the TV though I wouldn't leave one there with a toddler running around)? They are both aware of my feelings, but don't see the danger. I can't stand to think of my daughter staring at the TV mindlessly the way my niece who is the same age does.

I have not done any divination on this (mostly because I'm afraid I know the answer), but wanted to seek your (and the commentariat's) advice. I have almost a year to work with before the TV moratorium expires. Thank you.

A side note, I was reading an article the other day about the CDC withdrawing one of the covid tests because it couldn't differentiate between covid and the flu. I mentioned to one of my co-workers and before he could respond, a third co-worker turned suddenly and said, "You're one of them, one of the deniers? I can't talk to you, I can't talk to you. There's 600,000 people dead." He then walked out very quickly. He returned about fifteen minutes later and asked me a job related question and just like that our cordial work relationship was restored as if nothing had happened. It was very much like a switch flipped in him and he was running a script that had been triggered. Very interesting experience.

I haven't had full prophetic dreams but I do seem to be able to sense a general flow of things sometimes, but nothing amusing, I can do that much better while awake. It seems like a slow process for me though because my natal Moon is afflicted by a Mercury retrograde in the same house, you might have a natural talent.

The thing to keep in mind per JMG's advice is to learn how to tell the difference between the flow of astral currents coming from the Cosmos and your own imagery, fears, passions, tendencies, etc, coming from your personality. Whenever you encounter something in your dreams, see if you can tell where are the images coming from, if they have any connection to what you think it means and what it's trying to tell you. Things that are from the astral currents don't have much to do with you personally, things that come from you sometimes are related to past events, what you've seen in the news, what other people are thinking or forgotten memories, learning to tell the difference is they key it seems. So the best way to tell the difference is to know yourself pretty well. Regular discursive meditation is they way to do this. Oh, and keep a journal! That way you can look back and see what you got right and what you got wrong.

Lastly, when you go to sleep, see if you can remain attentive but relaxed while you start dozing of, that way you can have more control and more vivid dreams while in the dream state. If you can, if you are in the Northern hemisphere, avoid sleeping with your head pointing North, East is best, North-East is OK, West is fine, South if you must. In the Southern hemisphere is the other way around for North and South; avoiding South, SE being ok, etc.

I've found "Creative Dreaming" by Patricia Garfield helpful for specific exercises and techniques for increasing the number of dreams you remember and how much control you have during them, though if I recall, it's more New Agey than traditional occultism. One example of a usefully hands-on piece of advice: when writing in your dream journal, do it while still lying down, in the dark (if it's still night). To be able to write, place one finger on the top of the journal so that you can keep the line about the same distance from it, and then move down a set amount for the next line. Doing it that way has been way more effective than everything else I've tried (electronic journal, writing later in the day, turning the light on, getting up and writing in another room, and so on).

I've also heard good things about "Dreams: A Portal to the Source" by Edward Whitmont, which is supposed to be one of the top Jungian guides to dreams, but I haven't yet read the copy I picked up. My mom had Fox Geezer Syndrome and had Fox News going 14 hours a day. She got totally sucked into it. She knew nothing about what was going on in our town, but got mad at me once when I, an Ohio resident, didn’t know who the senator from Alaska was. Didn’t care, either, which probably compounded the offense. 😊. So to sun up, if you live in the U.S., I think you should have a TV but hardly ever use it.

I miss TV radios, a product once popular with women who had boring desk jobs. We all had one and some of the ladies I worked with had a whole slate of soap operas and talk shows to pass the time. (Keying, a field which I think is gone, was about as interesting as watching paint dry.). comments.title: Vaccine Tarot reading
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you for fielding these questions!

I have recently done a tarot reading where I asked whether I should get vaccinated or not. For context, in the spring I took this question very seriously. I did my research and then brought the topic into my prayer and meditation practice for several weeks. I did not use any divination at the time. At some point after discursive reasoning and sitting with the question it "settled" upon me that I would not get vaccinated. This conclusion had a quality of knowing and sureness of which I was not that familiar with. I then went on with my life with this decision made. Now as fall is approaching I have been under a bit of pressure from others about this choice, and see that this pressure could rise. I am aware that I have a personality sensitive to people perceiving me as a bad person. i.e perhaps a bit prone to taking this pressure too seriously.

In any case, I asked this question "should I get vaccinated?" after a few of these run ins, I pulled the World and as a support card Death. My sense from this reading is that it is confirming the decision I had come to during prayer and the world opening up from it. The Death card seems obviously inauspicious with respect to getting the vaccine.

Your thoughts on this reading much appreciated. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Vaccine Tarot reading
replies.content: I would interpret that reading exactly the same way you did. You have a choice to make between two paths. One leads to the world. The other -- well, you can fill in the blank yourself...
